title: Solar Set to Rule World’s Power Supply: Three Things to Know | BloombergNEF
gist: BloombergNEF's latest energy transition outlook projects solar will overtake coal as the world's leading electricity generator within six years, even though 2026 marks the first-ever slight year-on-year dip in installations; solar and batteries are set to transform power markets over the next decade, with revenue cannibalization and China's manufacturing dominance the key headwinds.

insights.claim: BloombergNEF projects solar will oust coal as the leading generator of electricity within six years.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: A hard timeline for solar surpassing coal reframes long-term generation-mix bets and thermal coal demand expectations.
insights.quote: Solar is set to oust coal as the leading generator of electricity within six years, BloombergNEF finds in its latest energy transition outlook, despite a first ever slight dip in installations in 2026.
insights.locator: Solar Set to Rule World's Power Supply

insights.claim: BNEF's Jenny Chase argues solar and batteries will transform world power markets in the next 10 years, ushering in an age where power is cheap whenever the sun shines.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Structurally cheap midday power reshapes utility, storage and industrial demand-response economics.
insights.quote: Solar and batteries will transform world power markets in the next 10 years

insights.claim: Global annual solar installations jumped from less than 100 gigawatts in 2016 to over 650 gigawatts last year, but BNEF sees 640 gigawatts this year, the first year-on-year dip, with growth resuming from 2027.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: The first-ever installation dip could pressure module makers and equipment suppliers near-term even as the long-term curve stays up.
insights.quote: The pace of global annual installations jumped from less than 100 gigawatts in 2016 to over 650 gigawatts last year. However, BNEF sees 640 gigawatts of solar installations this year, the first year-on-year dip.

insights.claim: As many as 46 markets around the world will install over a gigawatt of solar each in 2026.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Broadening geographic base of gigawatt-scale solar markets diversifies demand beyond a few dominant countries.
insights.quote: As many as 46 markets around the world will install over a gigawatt of solar each in 2026.

insights.claim: Fixed-axis PV is the cheapest source for new generation per BNEF's global levelized cost benchmarks, with onshore wind a close second, while coal costs almost double these two and even exceeds battery storage.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Coal being pricier than storage on an LCOE basis undermines the economic case for new coal builds globally.
insights.quote: Coal is almost double the cost of these cost-competitive two technologies, and is even higher than battery storage, which has seen the most dramatic fall in costs over the last few years.

insights.claim: Gas (combined cycle gas turbine) and offshore wind are the highest-cost generation technologies in BNEF's benchmarks.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Offshore wind and CCGT sitting at the top of the cost curve pressures returns for developers concentrated in those technologies.
insights.quote: The highest cost technologies are gas (combined cycle gas turbine) and offshore wind.

themes.theme: Market leaders and China concentration risk

insights.claim: China is by far the largest solar market, followed by India, the US and Germany, with Pakistan rounding out the top five and Brazil, Japan and Saudi Arabia in the next tier for 2026 installations.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: The emerging-market names in the top ranks (Pakistan, Brazil, Saudi Arabia) flag where new solar demand growth is concentrating.
insights.quote: China is the largest market for solar by far, followed by an eclectic list of countries with India, the US and Germany next in line

insights.claim: Concerns around solar growth center on China's dominance of equipment manufacturing, prompting many countries to launch incentives for domestic module assembly, cell and wafer manufacture, and polysilicon production.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Localization incentives create investable opportunities in non-China module, cell, wafer and polysilicon capacity.
insights.quote: Quite a few countries have launched incentive programs to encourage domestic assembly of modules, the manufacture of cells and wafers, or the production of polysilicon.

insights.claim: Revenue cannibalization is a growing issue as low power prices at peak solar generating periods hit developers' revenues, and while batteries mitigate it, their utilization falls at higher deployment levels which weakens their economic case.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Cannibalization plus declining battery utilization at scale caps merchant returns and argues for revenue-contracted or hybrid assets.
insights.quote: at higher levels of deployment, their utilization falls, which weakens their economic case.
insights.locator: Revenue cannibalization is also an issue

insights.claim: Trade barriers may slow solar progress in the specific markets that implement them, according to BNEF's Jenny Chase.
insights.speaker: BloombergNEF
insights.so_what: Tariff-imposing markets risk slower deployment and higher costs, a differentiator when comparing regional solar exposure.
insights.quote: though trade barriers may slow progress in markets that implement them
